01.04.08.05.001sa: What two broad categories of factors drive tissue aging?

## Factors Driving Tissue Aging
- Intrinsic factors: programmed genetic changes and accumulation of molecular damage over time
- Extrinsic factors: environmental exposures, lifestyle, and disease
- Aging changes are gradual, cumulative, and universal across all tissue types
- The interplay between these two categories determines the rate and severity of tissue decline in any individual
01.04.08.05.002sa: What are the two intrinsic factors that contribute to tissue aging?
Programmed genetic changes and accumulation of molecular damage over time

## Intrinsic Aging Factors
- Programmed genetic changes: built-in shifts in gene expression that occur over the lifespan
- Molecular damage accumulation: the gradual buildup of errors and damage at the DNA, protein, and organelle level
- These are distinguished from extrinsic factors (environmental exposures, lifestyle, disease) because they occur independently of outside influence
01.04.08.05.003sa: Name three extrinsic factors that contribute to tissue aging.

## Extrinsic Aging Factors
- Environmental exposures: UV radiation, pollution, toxins
- Lifestyle: diet, exercise habits, smoking, alcohol use
- Disease: chronic illness and repeated injury accelerate tissue decline
- These are contrasted with intrinsic factors (programmed genetic changes and molecular damage accumulation)
01.04.08.05.004sa: What happens to the rate of cell division in epithelial tissue with aging?

## Epithelial Aging
- Slowed cell division leads to thinner, less robust epithelial layers
- The basement membrane thickens and loses permeability
- In the skin specifically, the epidermis thins, the dermal-epidermal junction flattens, and collagen and elastic fibers in the dermis are lost
- The combined effect is slower wound healing, increased fragility, wrinkles, and sagging
01.04.08.05.005sa: What two changes occur in the basement membrane of epithelial tissue with aging?

## Basement Membrane Aging
- Thickening of the basement membrane reduces the efficiency of diffusion across it
- Loss of permeability impairs nutrient and waste exchange between the epithelium and underlying connective tissue
- This is significant because epithelial tissue is avascular and depends entirely on diffusion through the basement membrane for its metabolic needs
01.04.08.05.006sa: What happens to the dermal-epidermal junction in aging skin, and what consequence does this have?
It flattens, reducing the surface area for nutrient exchange

## Dermal-Epidermal Junction Aging
- In young skin, the junction has rete ridges (epidermal projections) and dermal papillae that interlock, creating a large surface area
- With aging, this junction flattens
- Reduced surface area means less efficient nutrient exchange between the vascular dermis and the avascular epidermis
- This contributes to the overall thinning and fragility of aged skin
01.04.08.05.007sa: What three visible skin changes result from the loss of collagen and elastic fibers in the dermis with aging?

## Dermal Fiber Loss
- Collagen loss: reduces tensile strength and structural support
- Elastic fiber loss: reduces the ability of skin to snap back after stretching
- The combined result is:
  1. Wrinkles (loss of structural support)
  2. Sagging (loss of elasticity)
  3. Increased fragility (thinner, weaker tissue more prone to tearing and bruising)
01.04.08.05.008sa: Why does wound healing slow in aging epithelial tissue?
Both epithelial regeneration and underlying connective tissue repair mechanisms become less efficient

## Wound Healing and Aging
- Epithelial regeneration slows because the rate of cell division decreases
- Connective tissue repair is also impaired due to reduced fibroblast activity, decreased collagen synthesis, and slower angiogenesis
- Both layers must cooperate for effective wound closure, so decline in either system delays the overall process
01.04.08.05.009sa: What are advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), and how do they affect connective tissue?
Molecules formed when sugars bind to and alter collagen, accelerating cross-linking and reducing flexibility

## Advanced Glycation End-Products (AGEs)
- Formed by non-enzymatic reactions between sugars and proteins (especially collagen)
- AGEs accelerate the formation of cross-links between collagen fibers
- Cross-linked collagen is stiffer and less flexible
- This process occurs gradually in everyone but is accelerated in conditions with elevated blood sugar (e.g., diabetes)
- AGEs affect connective tissues throughout the body, contributing to stiffness in tendons, ligaments, blood vessels, and skin
01.04.08.05.010sa: What process causes collagen fibers to become stiffer with aging?
Accumulation of cross-links between collagen fibers

## Collagen Cross-Linking
- Cross-links form between adjacent collagen molecules over time
- This is a normal process but accelerates with age and with exposure to AGEs
- Cross-linked collagen resists stretching and deformation
- The result is stiffer, less resilient connective tissue throughout the body
01.04.08.05.011sa: What two degenerative changes can occur in elastic fibers with aging?

## Elastic Fiber Aging
- Elastic fibers lose their ability to recoil
- Fragmentation: fibers break into smaller pieces, losing continuity
- Calcification: mineral deposits form within the fibers, making them rigid
- Both changes reduce the tissue's ability to stretch and return to its original shape
- This affects skin, blood vessels, lungs, and other elastic-fiber-rich tissues
01.04.08.05.012sa: What changes occur in ground substance with aging?
It decreases in volume and water content

## Ground Substance Aging
- Ground substance is the gel-like material that fills the space between cells and fibers in connective tissue
- With aging, its volume decreases and it loses water content
- This reduces the tissue's ability to:
  - Resist compression
  - Provide cushioning
  - Facilitate diffusion of nutrients and waste
- These changes contribute to joint stiffness, reduced shock absorption, and impaired tissue nutrition
01.04.08.05.013sa: What happens to articular cartilage with aging?
It thins and becomes more prone to damage

## Articular Cartilage Aging
- Articular cartilage covers the ends of bones at synovial joints
- With aging, it thins due to decreased chondrocyte activity and reduced proteoglycan content
- Thinner cartilage provides less cushioning and is more susceptible to mechanical damage
- This is a key factor in the development of osteoarthritis
01.04.08.05.014sa: Why do intervertebral discs lose height with aging, and what does this contribute to?
They lose water content and height, contributing to loss of stature

## Intervertebral Disc Aging
- The nucleus pulposus of intervertebral discs is rich in proteoglycans that attract and hold water
- With aging, proteoglycan content decreases, so the discs lose water and compress
- Reduced disc height across multiple vertebral levels results in measurable loss of overall stature
- Dehydrated discs are also less effective as shock absorbers and more prone to herniation
01.04.08.05.015sa: What happens to tendons and ligaments with aging?
They become stiffer and more prone to injury

## Tendon and Ligament Aging
- Collagen cross-linking increases stiffness
- Elastic fiber degradation reduces flexibility
- Ground substance water loss decreases resilience
- The combined effect is connective tissue that is less able to absorb sudden forces, making tears and ruptures more likely
01.04.08.05.016sa: What condition results when bone resorption by osteoclasts outpaces bone formation by osteoblasts with aging?

## Bone Mass and Aging
- Bone is constantly remodeled by osteoclasts (resorption) and osteoblasts (formation)
- In youth, these processes are balanced or favor formation
- With aging, resorption outpaces formation, leading to net bone loss
- Osteopenia: moderate reduction in bone density
- Osteoporosis: severe reduction in bone density with increased fracture risk
- Hormonal changes (especially declining estrogen) accelerate this imbalance
01.04.08.05.017sa: The progressive loss of skeletal muscle mass and performance with aging

## Sarcopenia
- Involves multiple concurrent changes:
  1. Progressive loss of skeletal muscle fibers
  2. Decrease in fiber size (atrophy)
  3. Shift from fast-twitch to slow-twitch fibers
  4. Replacement of lost muscle with adipose and fibrous tissue
- Results in reduced strength, power, and functional capacity
- Can be partially offset by resistance exercise and adequate protein intake
01.04.08.05.018sa: What term describes the progressive loss of skeletal muscle mass and performance with aging?

## Sarcopenia
- Not simply loss of muscle size, but a complex process involving:
  - Fiber loss (especially fast-twitch/Type II fibers)
  - Fiber atrophy
  - Fiber type shifting (fast-twitch to slow-twitch)
  - Infiltration of adipose and fibrous tissue into muscle
- Contributes to falls, frailty, and loss of independence in older adults
01.04.08.05.019sa: List the four components of sarcopenia.
Progressive loss of muscle fibers, decrease in fiber size, shift from fast-twitch to slow-twitch fibers, and replacement of muscle with adipose and fibrous tissue

## Four Components of Sarcopenia
1. Progressive loss of skeletal muscle fibers: total number of fibers decreases
2. Decrease in fiber size (atrophy): remaining fibers become smaller
3. Fiber type shift: fast-twitch (Type II) fibers are preferentially lost, shifting the ratio toward slow-twitch (Type I)
4. Tissue replacement: lost muscle is replaced by adipose (fat) and fibrous (scar) tissue
- The shift from fast-twitch to slow-twitch fibers specifically reduces power and speed, while the infiltration of non-contractile tissue further decreases force production
01.04.08.05.020sa: A wear-and-tear pigment consisting of undigested residues in lysosomes

## Lipofuscin
- A yellowish-brown pigment granule
- Composed of oxidized lipids, proteins, and other materials that lysosomes cannot fully degrade
- Accumulates in long-lived, postmitotic cells (especially cardiac muscle cells and neurons)
- Its presence increases with age and is used as a marker of cellular aging
- While it does not directly cause cell dysfunction, heavy accumulation may impair cellular function by occupying space within the cell
01.04.08.05.021sa: What term describes the wear-and-tear pigment that accumulates in cardiac muscle and neurons with aging?

## Lipofuscin
- Found primarily in cells that do not divide (postmitotic cells) because they cannot dilute the pigment through cell division
- Consists of undigested residues trapped in lysosomes
- Cardiac muscle and neurons are the classic sites of accumulation
- Its buildup is a hallmark of aging at the cellular level
01.04.08.05.022sa: What two changes in aging cardiac muscle make the heart stiffer and less compliant?

## Cardiac Muscle Aging
- Lipofuscin accumulation: fills cardiac muscle cells with indigestible residues
- Increased fibrosis: collagen deposition between cardiac muscle fibers stiffens the myocardium
- A stiffer heart has reduced diastolic filling (cannot relax and fill as easily)
- This reduces cardiac output and exercise tolerance in aging individuals
01.04.08.05.023sa: Stiffening of the arterial walls due to collagen accumulation, elastic fiber degradation, and calcification

## Arteriosclerosis
- Affects the smooth muscle layer of arterial walls
- Three contributing processes:
  1. Collagen accumulation: increases wall rigidity
  2. Elastic fiber degradation: reduces the ability of arteries to expand and recoil with each heartbeat
  3. Calcification: mineral deposits further harden the walls
- Results in increased systolic blood pressure and increased workload on the heart
- Distinct from atherosclerosis, which involves lipid plaque buildup within arterial walls
01.04.08.05.024sa: What term describes the stiffening of arterial walls due to collagen accumulation, elastic fiber degradation, and calcification?

## Arteriosclerosis
- A general term for arterial wall stiffening
- Involves changes in the smooth muscle and connective tissue layers of arteries
- Not the same as atherosclerosis (plaque formation), though the two often coexist
- Contributes to isolated systolic hypertension, a common finding in elderly individuals
01.04.08.05.025sa: List three changes that occur in aging nervous tissue at the cellular level.
Gradual loss of neurons, reduced synaptic density, and decreased neurotransmitter levels

## Nervous Tissue Aging
- Neuron loss: occurs particularly in specific brain regions, not uniformly
- Reduced synaptic density: fewer connections between remaining neurons
- Decreased neurotransmitter levels: less chemical signaling capacity
- Additional changes include lipofuscin accumulation and myelin deterioration
- These changes collectively contribute to declines in memory, processing speed, reflexes, and sensory acuity
01.04.08.05.026sa: What happens to myelin in aging nervous tissue, and what is the functional consequence?

## Myelin and Aging
- Myelin is the lipid-rich sheath that insulates axons and enables rapid saltatory conduction
- With aging, myelin sheaths thin, develop irregularities, and may partially break down
- Slower nerve conduction results in:
  - Delayed reflexes
  - Reduced processing speed
  - Impaired sensory acuity
- Myelin deterioration compounds the effects of neuron loss and reduced synaptic density
01.04.08.05.027sa: Protective caps on chromosomes that shorten with each cell division

## Telomeres
- Repetitive DNA sequences (TTAGGG in humans) at the ends of chromosomes
- They protect coding DNA from degradation during replication
- Shorten with each cell division because DNA polymerase cannot fully replicate the very end of a linear chromosome
- When telomeres become critically short, the cell enters cell cycle arrest or senescence
- The enzyme telomerase can rebuild telomeres but is largely inactive in most adult somatic cells
01.04.08.05.028sa: What happens to a cell when its telomeres become critically short?
The cell enters cell cycle arrest or senescence

## Telomere Shortening and Senescence
- Critically short telomeres trigger DNA damage response pathways
- The cell is forced into either:
  - Cell cycle arrest: the cell stops dividing permanently
  - Senescence: the cell remains alive but non-dividing and may secrete inflammatory mediators (SASP)
- This mechanism limits the replicative lifespan of somatic cells
- It serves as a tumor suppression mechanism but also contributes to tissue aging
01.04.08.05.029sa: Cells that have stopped dividing and secrete inflammatory mediators that contribute to tissue dysfunction

## Senescent Cells
- Cells that have permanently exited the cell cycle
- They remain metabolically active but do not divide
- They secrete a characteristic mix of inflammatory cytokines, growth factors, and proteases known as the SASP
- Accumulation of senescent cells is a hallmark of aging tissues
- Senolytic drugs are an emerging class of therapies designed to selectively clear senescent cells
01.04.08.05.030sa: What is the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP)?
The secretion of inflammatory mediators by senescent cells that contributes to tissue dysfunction

## SASP
- Senescent cells do not simply sit quietly; they actively secrete:
  - Inflammatory cytokines
  - Growth factors
  - Matrix metalloproteinases (proteases)
- This secretory profile:
  - Promotes chronic, low-grade inflammation in aging tissues
  - Can drive neighboring cells toward senescence
  - Disrupts normal tissue architecture and function
- SASP is a major research target because it links cellular senescence to systemic aging effects
01.04.08.05.031sa: What term describes the phenomenon in which senescent cells secrete inflammatory mediators that contribute to tissue dysfunction?

## SASP
- The defining behavioral feature of senescent cells
- Includes cytokines, chemokines, growth factors, and proteases
- Creates a pro-inflammatory microenvironment that damages surrounding healthy tissue
- Can induce senescence in neighboring cells, creating a spreading effect
- A key therapeutic target in the field of aging research
01.04.08.05.032sa: What happens to mitochondrial DNA with aging, and what are the two consequences?
Mitochondrial DNA progressively accumulates damage, leading to reduced energy production and increased production of reactive oxygen species

## Mitochondrial DNA and Aging
- Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) is more vulnerable to damage than nuclear DNA because it:
  - Lacks protective histones
  - Has limited repair mechanisms
  - Is located near the electron transport chain (a major source of reactive oxygen species)
- Accumulated damage leads to:
  1. Reduced energy production: dysfunctional mitochondria produce less ATP
  2. Increased ROS production: damaged mitochondria leak more free radicals, which cause further damage
- This creates a vicious cycle of mitochondrial decline
01.04.08.05.033sa: What two protein quality control systems decline in efficiency with aging?

## Protein Quality Control and Aging
- Proteasome: a molecular complex that tags and degrades misfolded or damaged proteins
- Autophagy: a process in which the cell encloses damaged proteins and organelles in vesicles and delivers them to lysosomes for degradation
- When these systems decline:
  - Damaged proteins accumulate
  - Dysfunctional organelles persist
  - Cellular efficiency drops
- The buildup of cellular debris contributes to lipofuscin accumulation and overall tissue dysfunction
01.04.08.05.034sa: List the four cellular-level mechanisms associated with aging in all tissue types.
Telomere shortening, accumulation of senescent cells, mitochondrial DNA damage, and declining protein quality control systems

## Four Cellular Mechanisms of Aging
1. Telomere shortening: limits replicative lifespan, triggers senescence
2. Senescent cell accumulation: non-dividing cells secrete SASP, promoting inflammation and tissue dysfunction
3. Mitochondrial DNA damage: reduces ATP production, increases reactive oxygen species
4. Declining protein quality control: proteasome and autophagy pathways become less efficient, allowing damaged proteins and organelles to accumulate
- These four mechanisms interact and reinforce each other, creating compounding decline across all tissue types
01.04.08.05.035sa: Emerging therapies that clear senescent cells

## Senolytic Drugs
- A class of drugs designed to selectively induce apoptosis in senescent cells
- The goal is to reduce the burden of SASP-producing cells in aging tissues
- By clearing senescent cells, these drugs may:
  - Reduce chronic inflammation
  - Improve tissue function
  - Slow or partially reverse aspects of tissue-level aging
- Still largely in experimental and early clinical trial stages
01.04.08.05.036sa: Name three lifestyle or therapeutic factors that can counteract tissue aging.
Exercise, adequate nutrition, and senolytic drugs
Move, eat well, and new medicine

## Counteracting Tissue Aging
- Exercise: preserves muscle mass (combats sarcopenia) and maintains cardiovascular function
- Adequate nutrition: supports extracellular matrix maintenance and immune function
- Senolytic drugs: an emerging therapy that clears senescent cells to reduce SASP-driven inflammation
- Tissue aging is not purely inevitable; these interventions can slow or partially reverse decline
- The degree of benefit depends on consistency, timing, and individual variation
01.04.08.05.037sa: In the context of connective tissue aging, what three changes occur in arterial cartilage, intervertebral discs, and bone mass respectively?
Articular cartilage thins, intervertebral discs lose water content and height, and bone mass decreases

## Connective Tissue Aging: Specific Structures
- Articular cartilage: thins and becomes more prone to damage, contributing to osteoarthritis
- Intervertebral discs: lose water content and height, contributing to loss of stature and reduced shock absorption
- Bone: resorption outpaces formation, leading to osteopenia and osteoporosis
- All three share the same underlying causes: collagen cross-linking, elastic fiber degradation, and ground substance loss
01.04.08.05.038sa: What four functional declines in the nervous system are commonly observed with aging?
Declines in memory, processing speed, reflexes, and sensory acuity

## Functional Nervous System Decline
- Memory: neuron loss and reduced synaptic density impair information storage and retrieval
- Processing speed: myelin deterioration slows signal transmission
- Reflexes: fewer neurons and slower conduction delay motor responses
- Sensory acuity: receptor cell loss and reduced neurotransmitter levels diminish perception
- These are the functional consequences of the structural changes (neuron loss, synaptic pruning, demyelination, neurotransmitter decline) that occur in aging nervous tissue
